title: A wolf, a sheep and cabbage

content.problemStatement:
The following is an easy game that the setter of this problem played when he was 8:

A boatman, a wolf, a sheep, and a cabbage are on the bank of a river. They have a small boat that is capable of carrying the boatman and at most one other animal/item with him. However, if left alone by the boatman, the wolf can eat the sheep, and the sheep can eat the cabbage. How can all four be moved safely to the opposite bank of the river?

Here is a nice visualization of the whole process in the original game. Disclaimer: writers, testers and CodeChef are not related to this link.

This leads to a more general problem. If there are other groups of animals/items with the boatman, is it possible to move them all to the opposite bank of the river in such a way that nobody/nothing gets eaten?

We will give you the number of animals/items (not including the boatman). Moreover, we will give you all a list of pairs of the form "**X Y**" where the **X**-th animal/item will be eaten by the **Y**-th one if they are both on the opposite bank to the boatman.

You are to determine **whether it is possible** to achieve the task or not.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two space-separated integers N and M, where N is the number of animals/items (not including the boatman) and M is the number of relations of the form 'X Y' where the X-th animal/item will be eaten by the Y-th one if they are both on the opposite bank to the boatman. The following M lines contain pairs of the form X Y.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print 'YES' if it is possible to move all animals/items to the opposite bank of the river in such a way that nobody/nothing gets eaten, and 'NO' otherwise.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The first example is the original version of the problem. The second example would have a solution if a boat could seat an additional animal/item.
There is no way to move all four animals to the opposite bank without any of them being eaten.
The boatman can move the first animal to the opposite bank, then move the second animal to the opposite bank, and finally move the first animal back to the original bank.

hidden.label: stress/general
hidden.expectedOutput:
YES
NO
YES
NO
YES
YES

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using a Breadth-First Search (BFS) algorithm. We start with the initial state where all animals/items are on the same bank as the boatman. Then, we generate all possible next states by moving the boatman and at most one animal/item to the opposite bank. We check if the new state is valid (i.e., no animal/item is eaten) and if it is, we add it to the queue. We repeat this process until we find a state where all animals/items are on the opposite bank or until the queue is empty.
solution.timeComplexity: O(2^N * M) per test case, O(T * 2^N * M) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(2^N) — the dominant structure is the queue used for BFS.
